EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system positioned over the Great Basin on May 14, 2019 forced strong southwest winds aloft over New Mexico. Sufficient mid level moisture was present in the atmosphere for isolated showers and thunderstorms to develop over central and eastern New Mexico. Most of this activity produced little to no rainfall with very gusty outflow winds. A dry microburst impacted the community of Alto during the early afternoon and resulted in damage to one home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was partially torn off a home in Alto due to high winds from a dry microburst. No other homes in the area were affected. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system over the Great Basin forced strong southwest winds aloft over New Mexico on May 17, 2019. Deepening surface low pressure over eastern New Mexico combined with stronger winds aloft to produce widespread strong southwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 45 mph. A localized wind gust up to 58 mph was reported at the Smokey Bears RAWS southwest of Ruidoso.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust up to 58 mph was reported by the Smokey Bear RAWS southwest of Ruidoso.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm system approaching New Mexico from the west interacted with deep moisture and very unstable conditions along a sharp dryline over eastern New Mexico on May 26, 2019. Dry showers and thunderstorms with strong downburst winds first developed along the Continental Divide by late morning before marching east into the Rio Grande Valley through the afternoon. Several locations within the Rio Grande Valley reported wind gusts in excess of 60 mph, including Los Lunas, Albuquerque, Santa Fe, and Taos. Wind damage was also reported as this activity moved across the central mountain chain into the Estancia Valley. Meanwhile, showers and thunderstorms fired up along the dryline in eastern New Mexico then quickly became severe with large hail, high winds, and several tornados. A violent supercell thunderstorm that moved through Union County during the late afternoon produced at least two tornadoes and three inch hail. Severe thunderstorms then evolved into a quasi-linear convective system over the Caprock region and resulted in a large area of high winds and large hail. Several more tornadoes developed along the southern edge of this line, including a spectacular wedge tornado near Dora. In addition to all the severe thunderstorms that impacted the region, many areas outside of thunderstorm activity in central and western New Mexico also reported strong southwest winds with gusts of 50 to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow wind gusts up to 60 mph were reported in Los Lunas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front that moved southwest through eastern New Mexico on July 4, 2019 set the stage for numerous showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall and isolated severe weather on July 5, 2019. Showers and thunderstorms first developed around the Pecos River Valley during the early afternoon before spreading northeast across the remainder of the eastern plains. The strongest cells produced torrential rainfall, strong winds, hail, and frequent lightning. A severe thunderstorm west of Clayton produced numerous funnel clouds and a weak tornado near Heringa Ranch. Golf ball size hail was also reported around Des Moines and Moses. Torrential rainfall around Fort Sumner resulted in minor flooding across town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rancher several miles southwest of Mount Dora reported a weak tornado on the ground for several minutes. Storm chasers in the area confirmed the tornado was more like a landspout with several funnel clouds observed during its evolution.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions across portions of western and central New Mexico at the end of April 2019 continued to improve in May. Drought conditions improved to zero percent of the state with severe or worse drought conditions. About 36 percent of the state remains abnormally dry to moderate drought conditions. Precipitation amounts for May 2019 ranged from around half an inch to an inch and a half across central and eastern New Mexico and two to three inches for northern parts of the state. The month of May was active across the state as several storm systems brought rain and even late season mountain snow to portions of northern and central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from April 2019 improved to moderate drought in May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front shifting southwest through northeastern New Mexico interacted with abundant moisture and instability beneath an upper level high to generate slow-moving showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall on July 28, 2019. A round of heavy rainfall over the Ute Park burn scar on July 27th set the stage for an elevated flash flood threat on the 28th. A strong thunderstorm that developed along the U.S. Highway 64 corridor between Cimarron and Ute Park led to flash flooding along the highway around 3 pm. Swift flowing water, rocks, mud, and debris forced the closure of part of the roadway for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding impacted the U.S. Highway 64 corridor between Cimarron and Ute Park. Portions of the roadway were closed for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple days of severe weather over west Texas assisted deep low level moisture return into eastern New Mexico on May 25, 2019. A potent mid level dry slot moving northeast ahead of an approaching upper level low pressure system over the western United States allowed the dryline to sharpen over the far eastern plains during the early afternoon. Several thunderstorms that moved northeast through Curry and Quay counties produced torrential rainfall, strong winds, and severe hail. The area around Clovis was the hardest hit with one to three inches of rainfall and quarter to half dollar size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of nickels reported on the east side of Clovis.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving winter storm that pummeled Arizona with record heavy snowfall on February 21, 2019 shifted east into New Mexico on the 22nd and 23rd. A large band of snow and associated Pacific cold front moved into the Gallup and Farmington areas late on the 21st before drifting east across the Continental Divide and into the Rio Grande Valley through the 22nd. Gallup picked up 10 to 15 inches of snowfall in less than 12 hours with blowing snow and low visibility. Interstate 40 was closed for several hours due to very dangerous travel conditions and several accidents. Snow and wind then slammed the Rio Grande Valley by the evening of the 22nd with widespread reports of two to six inches of heavy wet snow from Albuquerque to Santa Fe. Snow continued to pummel the northern mountains through the early morning hours of the 23rd, leaving behind a whopping 18 to 24 inches of fresh snow. Several locations across northern and central New Mexico established new daily snowfall records. The storm system then exited into the eastern plains where snow, blowing snow, low visibility, and icy roadways resulted in the closure of U.S. 64 from Clayton to Raton and Interstate 25 within Raton Pass. Strong winds in the wake of this system produced wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph across the eastern plains along with areas of blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various sources in the high terrain east of Santa Fe reported between 12 and 24 inches of snow. Difficult travel was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions across portions of western and central New Mexico at the end of January 2019 improved only slightly in February. Severe drought conditions slightly improved to 32 percent of the state, of which around 13 percent was deemed extreme drought or worse. Exceptional drought improved to less than 1 percent of New Mexico. Precipitation amounts for February 2019 ranged from around less than one half inch across the east to three to four inches for parts of northern and northwest New Mexico. Several winter storm systems that moved across the state in February continued to bring much needed snowfall to portions of northern and central New Mexico. However, conditions were drier than normal in the southeast, and portions of the region became abnormally dry in February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme to exceptional drought conditions from January 2019 continued through February. Far western portions of the area improved to severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful jet stream sliding over New Mexico ahead of an approaching storm system from southern California delivered a bout of strong to damaging winds to parts of northeastern New Mexico on February 13-14, 2019. Southwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 55 mph developed along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains into the eastern plains on the 13th. Periodic high winds then focused in the area from the Raton airport to Cimarron, Rociada, and Las Vegas during the overnight hours and morning of the 14th. The strongest winds occurred around Raton where a peak wind gust up to 84 mph was reported. High winds slowly tapered off by noon across the entire area. A carport was destroyed in Cimarron, another was blown onto the roof of a home near Las Vegas, and two semi trailers were toppled on Interstate 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station near Tecolotito reported a peak wind gust up to 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast westerly flow aloft in the wake of a strong winter storm system from February 19, 2019 allowed mountain wave activity to produce localized high wind gusts along the central mountain chain. Relatively tranquil winds were reported across a vast majority of the state, however Angel Fire and Clines Corners both saw peak wind gusts near 60 mph. Winds were strong enough to cause blowing snow across several roadways within the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and far northeastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Angel Fire airport reported a peak wind gust up to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system passed north of the region with a jet streak clipping southern NM and West Texas resulting in a period of high winds through the Guadalupe Mountains.

A slow-moving winter storm that pummeled Arizona with record heavy snowfall on February 21, 2019 shifted east into New Mexico on the 22nd and 23rd. A large band of snow and associated Pacific cold front moved into the Gallup and Farmington areas late on the 21st before drifting east across the Continental Divide and into the Rio Grande Valley through the 22nd. Gallup picked up 10 to 15 inches of snowfall in less than 12 hours with blowing snow and low visibility. Interstate 40 was closed for several hours due to very dangerous travel conditions and several accidents. Snow and wind then slammed the Rio Grande Valley by the evening of the 22nd with widespread reports of two to six inches of heavy wet snow from Albuquerque to Santa Fe. Snow continued to pummel the northern mountains through the early morning hours of the 23rd, leaving behind a whopping 18 to 24 inches of fresh snow. Several locations across northern and central New Mexico established new daily snowfall records. The storm system then exited into the eastern plains where snow, blowing snow, low visibility, and icy roadways resulted in the closure of U.S. 64 from Clayton to Raton and Interstate 25 within Raton Pass. Strong winds in the wake of this system produced wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph across the eastern plains along with areas of blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various sources along the Continental Divide reported between 5 and 10 inches of snow. Interstate 40 was closed along the Continental Divide for several hours due to extreme travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extended stretch of severe weather continued to impact eastern New Mexico on June 2, 2019 as more dryline thunderstorms shifted northeast through the region. A slow-moving upper level storm system to the west of New Mexico continued to pump deep moisture into the region with strong afternoon heating and instability leading to scattered to numerous showers and storms. Thunderstorms first developed within the Rio Grande Valley and nearby higher terrain during the early afternoon before shifting onto the high plains of eastern New Mexico. A cluster of thunderstorms that developed near Los Lunas moved northeast across the east mountain communities into the Santa Fe area with hail up to the size of golf balls. Large hail, strong winds, very heavy rainfall, and even a tornado were reported with these storms as they progressed into eastern New Mexico through the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of ping pong balls on the east side of Los Lunas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system responsible for nearly a week of repeated rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms finally began to weaken west of the area and lessen its grip on New Mexico by June 5, 2019. Enough low level moisture and instability was still in place over far eastern New Mexico to develop isolated showers and thunderstorms with hail and high winds. The strongest storms impacted the Caprock region with wind gusts near 65 mph around Clovis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second thunderstorm produced hail up to the size of nickels a few miles southwest of Portales.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface boundary sagged southwest into far northeastern New Mexico on June 7, 2019 while a weak upper level wave ejected northeast through the southern Rockies. Enough moisture and instability was present to allow a couple thunderstorms to develop during the evening hours over Colfax and Union counties. The strongest of these storms impacted the area around Capulin with wind gusts around 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust estimated up to 60 mph occurred with a thunderstorm in Capulin. Torrential rainfall was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent back door cold front surged south through eastern New Mexico with high sustained winds across Curry County on June 9, 2019. North winds of 35 to 45 mph impacted the area during the morning hours. Travelers along U.S. 60 noted several small tree limbs were down along the road between Clovis and Texico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds between 43 mph and 46 mph were reported at Cannon AFB and the Clovis airport for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was centered over southern California with upper level disturbances moving over West Texas and southeast New Mexico. There was a surface trough/dryline along the extreme West Texas/eastern New Mexico border with very moist and unstable air to the east. The wind shear across the area was also good. These conditions resulted in a storm that produced large hail in southeast New Mexico.

An upper low was over the Arizona/Mexico border. There was a moisture rich boundary layer across far West Texas. Good moisture and instability were in place across southeast New Mexico and West Texas. These conditions resulted in a thunderstorm producing a tornado in southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Eddy County and produced a tornado 15 miles south southwest of Hope. The tornado briefly developed over far western Eddy County over an open field and no damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over New Mexico and provided lift over southeast New Mexico and West Texas. Good wind shear and instability were present across the area. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms that produced large hail and wind damage across southeast New Mexico and West Texas.

An upper level low was over New Mexico and provided lift over southeast New Mexico and West Texas. Good wind shear and instability were present across the area. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms that produced large hail and wind damage across southeast New Mexico and West Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Lea County and produced a 70 mph wind gust in Hobbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system centered over southern California drew abundant low level moisture into the eastern plains of New Mexico on June 1, 2019. Deep moisture combined with an unstable atmosphere allowed storms to first develop along a sharp dryline over central New Meixco. The storms quickly became severe as they moved northeast with large hail and high wind gusts. Quarter sized hail was reported across portions of eastern New Mexico, including Guadalupita, Clayton, Picacho, and Roswell, and brief strong wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were recorded across Curry and Roosevelt counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported west of Roswell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts tore off a large section of roofing from a barn in Melrose. A nearby Melrose RAWS site recorded a peak wind gust of 61 mph. Damage amounts are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level storm system responsible for the extended stretch of severe weather starting in late May 2019 produced yet another round of severe thunderstorms over parts of eastern New Mexico on June 3, 2019. The deeper low level moisture and instability started creeping farther east compared to recent days so the stronger storms were over far northeastern and far southeastern New Mexico. Large hail was reported once again with Lincoln and Chaves counties along with high winds and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A virga bomb produced a downburst wind gust up to 68 mph at the Raton airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weak upper level wave responsible for all the severe thunderstorms on June 17, 2019 exited New Mexico on June 18, 2019. Just enough moisture and instability was left over across parts of the northern high terrain and the northeastern plains to produce isolated strong to severe thunderstorms. The strongest storms impacted the area around Union County, however one storm that moved over Espanola was able to drop hail up to the size of half dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving thunderstorm produced quarter to half dollar size hail around Espanola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture in place across New Mexico interacted with a weak upper level wave moving through the southern Rockies to produce isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms on June 27, 2019. Thunderstorms first developed within the Rio Grande Valley during the early afternoon before moving east into the eastern plains of New Mexico. Several of the storms within central New Mexico were strong with gusty winds, heavy rainfall, and small hail through the afternoon. Storms intensified further as they moved into eastern New Mexico where high winds, hail, torrential rainfall, and even a funnel cloud were reported. The strongest storms impacted Roosevelt and Curry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Portales airport reported a peak wind gust up to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry and unstable airmass in place over western New Mexico on June 29, 2019 interacted with a weak layer of mid level moisture to produce isolated virga showers with brief strong winds. The strongest downburst winds occurred around Gallup where a peak wind gust up to 61 mph was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gallup airport reported a peak downburst wind gust up to 61 mph from nearby virga showers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was over the Great Lakes region with a broad ridge over West Texas and southeast New Mexico. Good moisture, instability, and wind shear were across southeast New Mexico. These conditions, along with a nearby upper level disturbance, resulted in thunderstorms with large hail and strong winds.

These conditions, along with a nearby upper level disturbance, resulted in thunderstorms with large hail and strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave moving east through the southern Rockies forced fast westerly flow aloft through central and eastern New Mexico on June 13, 2019. Scattered showers and thunderstorms first developed over Socorro County where hail up to the size of quarters accumulated along Interstate 25. The deeper moisture and instability focused over eastern New Mexico during the late afternoon and evening hours where several thunderstorms produced large hail, especially over Harding County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail several miles northeast of Dunken.

A cluster of thunderstorms that developed near Los Lunas moved northeast across the east mountain communities into the Santa Fe area with hail up to the size of golf balls. Large hail, strong winds, very heavy rainfall, and even a tornado were reported with these storms as they progressed into eastern New Mexico through the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail in Los Lunas.

The area around Lincoln and Chaves counties were hammered once again with large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple injuries were reported at the Guru Ram Das Puri retreat center just west of Espa��ola due to a large tent that blew down during a dust devil. The tent was occupied by over 300 people, according to Rio Arriba County Sheriff's deputies. Powerful winds from the dust devil ripped out posts and tore apart ropes used to secure the large tent. Multiple agencies responded, including units from Espa��ola Fire Rescue, Rio Arriba County, and Santa Fe County Fire Department. Multiple ambulances from Presbyterian hospital and a helicopter arrived quickly at the scene. About 20 patients were treated at Presbyterian Espanola Hospital for non life-threatening injuries. One of the patients was transported to Albuquerque for critical head injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty people were injured when a dust devil blew apart a large tent at a summer solstice event at the Guru Ram Das Puri retreat center west of Espanola. Damage amounts are a rough estimate for destruction of the event tent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system responsible for nearly a week of repeated rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms finally began to weaken west of the area and lessen its grip on New Mexico by June 5, 2019. Enough low level moisture and instability was still in place over far eastern New Mexico to develop isolated showers and thunderstorms with hail and high winds. The strongest storms impacted the Caprock region with wind gusts near 65 mph around Clovis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail reported 22 miles east-northeast of Roy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave moving east through the southern Rockies forced fast westerly flow aloft through central and eastern New Mexico on June 13, 2019. Scattered showers and thunderstorms first developed over Socorro County where hail up to the size of quarters accumulated along Interstate 25. The deeper moisture and instability focused over eastern New Mexico during the late afternoon and evening hours where several thunderstorms produced large hail, especially over Harding County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail a few miles south of Rosebud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast northwest flow in place over northeastern New Mexico on June 15, 2019 interacted with sufficient moisture and instability to produce a few showers and thunderstorms along the east slopes of the central mountain chain. The strongest storm moved southeast across Raton with light accumulations of nickel size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of nickels accumulated up to one inch in Raton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active spring weather pattern brought another round of severe thunderstorms to eastern New Mexico on June 17, 2019. A weak upper level wave moving slowly east through the region combined with deep atmospheric moisture and strong instability to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms. Thunderstorms first developed along the central mountain chain during the early afternoon before pushing east across the eastern plains through the late evening hours. Large hail was the main impact once again with many spotters reporting quarter to ping pong ball size hail. Very heavy rainfall with these storms also led to flash flooding over parts of Roosevelt County. A tornado was also captured near Encino.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail with heavy rainfall and minor flooding around Mora and Cleveland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was over the Southern Plains with an upper level trough over the west coast. An upper level disturbance moved over southeast New Mexico and West Texas. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms that produced hail in southeast New Mexico.

An upper level low pressure system responsible for nearly a week of repeated rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms finally began to weaken west of the area and lessen its grip on New Mexico by June 5, 2019. Enough low level moisture and instability was still in place over far eastern New Mexico to develop isolated showers and thunderstorms with hail and high winds. The strongest storms impacted the Caprock region with wind gusts near 65 mph around Clovis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of nickels a few miles southwest of Portales.

An upper level storm system centered over southern California drew abundant low level moisture into the eastern plains of New Mexico on June 1, 2019. Deep moisture combined with an unstable atmosphere allowed storms to first develop along a sharp dryline over central New Meixco. The storms quickly became severe as they moved northeast with large hail and high wind gusts. Quarter sized hail was reported across portions of eastern New Mexico, including Guadalupita, Clayton, Picacho, and Roswell, and brief strong wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were recorded across Curry and Roosevelt counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail reported in Guadalupita.

An upper level low was over New Mexico and provided lift over southeast New Mexico and West Texas. Good wind shear and instability were present across the area. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms that produced large hail and wind damage across southeast New Mexico and West Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Lea County and produced wind damage in Hobbs. A power pole was downed by winds in central Hobbs at the intersection of Sanger and Shipp. A large tree was blown over by the wind. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

A dryline was backed up against the mountains of West Texas and southeast New Mexico with very moist air to the east of this feature. Good instability and wind shear were present. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms that produced large hail in West Texas and southeast New Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active spring weather pattern brought another round of severe thunderstorms to eastern New Mexico on June 17, 2019. A weak upper level wave moving slowly east through the region combined with deep atmospheric moisture and strong instability to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms. Thunderstorms first developed along the central mountain chain during the early afternoon before pushing east across the eastern plains through the late evening hours. Large hail was the main impact once again with many spotters reporting quarter to ping pong ball size hail. Very heavy rainfall with these storms also led to flash flooding over parts of Roosevelt County. A tornado was also captured near Encino.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second storm produced hail up to the size of half dollars near Cannon AFB.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moving through the Rockies resulted in high winds through the Guadalupe Mountains.
An upper level storm system passing to the north of the area resulted in high winds in the Guadalupe and Delaware Mountains.

The dryline was backed up to the mountains of West Texas and southeast New Mexico. An upper level disturbance moved over the region which provided additional lift. Good instability and wind shear were in place. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms producing large hail across southeast New Mexico and West Texas.

Upper low was moving into southeastern Arizona with moderately deep low level moisture pushing west toward the Rio Grande.  A disturbance in the southwest flow across the Borderland helped to fire off thunderstorms across northern Otero county into northern Dona Ana county.  Everything from severe hail to a flash flood and tornado were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm survey showed a mobile home was completely destroyed with 3 other residences receiving moderate damage due mainly to flying debris. Several out buildings including sheds and barns were either completely destroyed or heavily damaged.  One out building was shifted off it's foundation and moved approximately 9 feet north.  A metal silo used for storage was lifted off it's foundation and traveled airborne for approximately 400 feet before striking one of the residential structures.

A very moist and unstable airmass in place along the Front Range of the Rockies on August 13, 2019 set the stage for an active night of severe weather over northeastern New Mexico through the morning hours of August 14, 2019. A large complex of showers and thunderstorms first developed over eastern Colorado before shifting southward down the eastern plains of New Mexico. The strongest storms impacted the area from near Clayton southward to Nara Visa where hail, high winds, frequent lightning, and torrential rainfall were reported. The most significant impacts occurred around Nara Visa where quarter size hail, severe winds, and three inches of rainfall resulted in damage to roofs and trees in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds and large hail resulted in roof damage to at least one home in Nara Visa. Damage amounts are a rough estimate for one home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and instability in place across New Mexico on August 9, 2019 set the stage for scattered showers and thunderstorms with torrential rainfall. Most of the storm activity produced locally heavy rainfall, gusty winds, small hail, and frequent lightning in the area from near Socorro to Ruidoso and the Caprock region of eastern New Mexico. The strongest storm impacted Clovis where a peak wind gust up to 66 mph was reported at the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clovis airport reported a peak wind gust up to 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the early afternoon of August 7, 2019. This activity moved slowly east along the U.S. Highway 64 corridor near Ute Park, resulting in flash flooding on the Ute Park burn scar. A significant surge of water, boulders, mud, and debris flowed down Turkey Creek and over-topped U.S. 64 between mile markers 303 and 304. NMDOT crews quickly cleared the roadway after flooding subsided. No damage was noted to the roadway or the bridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant surge of flood waters with boulders, mud, and debris impacted U.S. Highway 64 at Turkey Creek. No damage was noted to the roadway or the bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high over the southwest trapped some deeper moisture over the region with slow moving thunderstorms developing on the west slopes of the Sacramento Mountains. Thunderstorms continued around the Alamogordo area for several hours with over 2 inches of rain reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple road closures were required around Alamogordo due to several inches of rain falling in and around the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low around the Rio Grande Valley combined with a weak disturbance moving through northwest flow aloft to produce a severe thunderstorm around the Oliver Lee State Park area during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to an inch and a half in diameter broke 10 skylights at a park facility at Oliver Lee State Park.  There was also some hail dents on park vehicles and some light debris on Dog Canyon Road from the heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from thunderstorms the night before across the Texas panhandle had moved into West Texas and southeast New Mexico. The atmosphere was unstable and fairly moist. These conditions contributed to thunderstorms with hail across southeast New Mexico.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds damaged a home several miles northwest of Nara Visa. Windows were blown out of the home and trees were toppled in the area. Damage amounts are a rough estimate for roof and window damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an extended period of dry and hot weather over most of New Mexico, a weak back door cold front delivered better moisture and cooler temperatures to eastern New Mexico. A few storms fired up over the higher terrain of southern New Mexico as well as the northeast plains on the afternoon of August 22, 2019. The strongest storm impacted the area around Capulin where quarter size hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail at the Capulin Volcano National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deeper moisture and instability in place behind a weak back door cold front set the stage for scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms over eastern New Mexico on August 23, 2019. Storms first fired up over northeastern New Mexico before moving southeast toward the Interstate 40 corridor through the evening hours. Locally heavy rainfall around Clayton led to minor flooding with lots of pea size hail. A line of strong thunderstorms that moved into Tucumcari produced wind gusts up to 50 mph. Another line of storms that moved across Roosevelt County produced severe wind gusts up to 68 mph at the Melrose Bombing Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Melrose Bombing Range reported peak wind gusts up to 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over the Four Corners region and an upper trough was strengthening over the eastern part of the country. A cold front that was associated with the upper trough was moving through West Texas and southeast New Mexico. This front interacted with the plentiful moisture across the area. These conditions resulted in flash flooding across southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Eddy County and produced flash flooding two miles south of Carlsbad. A spotter reported  two inches of rain in about an hour with flooding of low lying areas. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over the Four Corners region and an upper trough was strengthening over the eastern part of the country. A cold front that was associated with the upper trough was moving through West Texas and southeast New Mexico. This front interacted with the plentiful moisture across the area. These conditions resulted in flash flooding across southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Eddy County and produced flash flooding in Carlsbad. There was curb to curb flooding on many streets in Carlsbad. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture intrusion moved west into central New Mexico behind a shallow back door cold front on August 27, 2019. Strong afternoon heating and instability led to a few showers and thunderstorms around the higher terrain. Weak steering flow allowed outflow boundaries to dominate storm development in the lower elevations through the late afternoon hours. A thunderstorm that developed over Rio Rancho shortly after 4 pm moved south over Intel and into the Cottonwood Mall area. The greatest impact from this storm was penny to quarter size hail near the Sandoval/Bernalillo county line. Another cluster of storms that developed west of Albuquerque during the late evening hours produced torrential rainfall, hail, gusty winds, and frequent cloud to ground lightning strikes. Laguna Pueblo reported water flowing out of Arroyo Colorado across Reservation Road 55.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail at Unser and Southern Boulevards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and instability in place over eastern New Mexico with easterly, upslope flow produced isolated showers and thunderstorms with torrential rainfall and large hail on August 28, 2019. Thunderstorms first developed over the Ruidoso area around 4 pm where minor flooding was reported from rainfall amounts of two to three inches in less than one hour. Antecedent conditions were fairly dry in the area which limited a more serious flash flood threat. The focus for strong to severe thunderstorms shifted into northeastern New Mexico by 5 pm where a couple storms produced small hail and torrential rainfall. The strongest storm impacted the area around Abbott with quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two public weather stations in Ruidoso reported rainfall rates near 10 inches per hour from a strong thunderstorm moving slowly east through the area. One station picked up 3.48 inches of rainfall in less than one hour, which fortunately only led to minor flooding on the Rio Ruidoso.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The axis of monsoon moisture shifted over central New Mexico on August 2, 2019 while a weak boundary moved southwest through the eastern plains. The combination of deep moisture, strong heating, and abundant instability with weak steering flow led to scattered showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall and strong outflow winds. Wet microbursts impacted the area around Clayton and Moriarty with peak wind gusts near 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet microburst produced a peak wind gust up to 61 mph at Stanley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a weak upper trough across the western part of the country, and a cold front was pushing through southeast New Mexico. Plentiful moisture, lift, and instability, were across the area. These conditions, along with previous rainfall, lead to heavy rain and flooding across southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain moved across Eddy County and produced flooding west of Artesia. There was water over the road along Highway 82 about 15 miles west of Artesia.

CZ_NAME: JOHNSON AND BARTLETT MESAS INCLUDING RATON PASS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level storm system over the Pacific northwest moved slowly southeast into the Four Corners region on October 24, 2019. Strong lift associated with the upper level low pressure system interacted with a moist, bitter cold air mass that shifted south down the front range of the Rockies. Light to moderate snow developed over northeast New Mexico and the Sangre de Cristo Mountains where total accumulations averaged three to six inches. The combination of record cold temperatures and snow led to difficult driving conditions across much of northeast New Mexico, including the closure of Interstate 40 at Raton Pass. Freezing drizzle and freezing fog were also reported across parts of eastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of two to six inches with freezing fog and low visibility resulted in severe winter driving conditions, including the closure of Interstate 25 between Raton and Raton Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough was moving through the Four Corners region.  An upper level jet of almost 100 knots was located over New Mexico with another jet diving down the back side of the trough. Several strong thunderstorms developed over Luna county with one able to produce a brief landspout tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture was submitted via social media from a weather spotter showing a brief tornado northeast of Hermanas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moving slowly east through southern New Mexico on October 8, 2019 interacted with sufficient moisture and instability to produce isolated showers and thunderstorms. A few of these thunderstorms were strong with small hail, gusty winds, frequent lightning, and heavy rainfall. One of these strong storms produced two inches of rainfall in Fort Sumner in less than one hour. A severe thunderstorm moved east across U.S. 285 south of Roswell in Chaves County while another impacted the area along U.S. 380 near San Antonio within Socorro County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail and 50 mph wind gusts impacted Midway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moving slowly east through southern New Mexico on October 8, 2019 interacted with sufficient moisture and instability to produce isolated showers and thunderstorms. A few of these thunderstorms were strong with small hail, gusty winds, frequent lightning, and heavy rainfall. One of these strong storms produced two inches of rainfall in Fort Sumner in less than one hour. A severe thunderstorm moved east across U.S. 285 south of Roswell in Chaves County while another impacted the area along U.S. 380 near San Antonio within Socorro County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters fell a couple miles east of San Antonio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second unseasonably strong upper level storm system over the Pacific northwest moved quickly southeast into the Four Corners region on October 29, 2019. Strong lift associated with the upper level low pressure system interacted with a moist, bitter cold air mass that shifted south down the front range of the Rockies. Widespread low clouds, freezing drizzle, freezing fog, low visibility, and light snowfall developed over eastern New Mexico and the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. Total ice accumulations of near one tenth of an inch impacted much of eastern New Mexico along with one to three inches of snow. Locally higher amounts up to five inches were reported around Angel Fire and Eagle Nest. The combination of more record cold temperatures with ice and snow led to difficult driving conditions across much of northeast New Mexico. U.S. Highway 64 was closed between Clayton and Raton due to very icy conditions and at least two crashes, one near Clayton and another near Des Moines. Interstate 40 was closed along a section between Newkirk and Tucumcari where at least one semi trailer overturned and another vehicle left the roadway.  The northbound lane of U.S. Highway 285 was closed near Vaughn due to very icy travel and at least one vehicle accident. Interstate 25 was also closed due to multiple vehicle crashes in black ice. The number of vehicle crashes was estimated at eight and the extent of any injuries was unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several lanes of Interstate 40 were closed between Newkirk and Tucumcari due to severe winter travel conditions. At least one vehicle accident and a semi rollover were reported. Damage amounts and the extent of any injuries was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions across portions of western New Mexico at the end of September 2019 expanded to include northern New Mexico in October. Moderate to severe drought conditions expanded to include 35 percent of the state of which 9 percent across western NM was deemed as severe drought. Abnormally dry to moderate drought conditions in southeastern NM at the end of September improved to no drought conditions in October as the remnants of Tropical Storm Narda moved through the area. Precipitation amounts across western NM during October ranged from a quarter to a half inch, however, dismal precipitation amounts continued in the Four Corners region where less than a quarter inch of precipitation was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from September 2019 continued in October.

Abnormally dry to moderate drought conditions in southeastern NM at the end of September improved to no drought conditions in October as the remnants of Tropical Storm Narda moved through the area. Precipitation amounts across western NM during October ranged from a quarter to a half inch, however, dismal precipitation amounts continued in the Four Corners region where less than a quarter inch of precipitation was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed during October 2019. Below normal precipitation amounts set the stage for deteriorating drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of tropical convection along the west coast of Mexico during the final days of September 2019 organized into Tropical Storm Narda through October 1, 2019. Meanwhile, an upper level low pressure system shifted south along the west coast of the United States allowing atmospheric flow to become southerly over the desert southwest. A record high level of atmospheric moisture for the month of October that surged north into New Mexico in this pattern led to widespread showers and thunderstorms with torrential rainfall over southeast New Mexico. One day rainfall totals averaged two to four inches with locally heavier amounts in excess of six inches from southern Chaves County northeastward into Roosevelt and Curry counties. Flash flooding impacted much of southern Chaves County where communities along U.S. 285 were inundated by flood waters. Flash flooding also occurred around Clovis and Portales as playa lakes overflowed onto nearby roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall over southern Chaves County led to flash flooding and large areas of ponding water over many roadways from near Dexter south to Hagerman. An observer a few miles west of Lake Arthur along Walnut Creek reported a whopping 6.50 of rain in 24 hours. State Road 2 was closed between Darby and Orchard Park roads, State Road 267 was closed between mile markers 11 and 15, U.S. Highway 285 was closed at Chikashaw Road, and State Road 409 was closed along the entire section leading to Bottomless State Park due to falling rocks and high water. Several roads were also closed throughout the community of Dexter. At least two vehicles were inundated in flood waters and motorists had to be rescued by emergency personnel. Fortunately, no one was injured during this flooding event. Damage amounts are estimated for total losses to two vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second unseasonably strong upper level storm system over the Pacific northwest moved quickly southeast into the Four Corners region on October 29, 2019. Strong lift associated with the upper level low pressure system interacted with a moist, bitter cold air mass that shifted south down the front range of the Rockies. Widespread low clouds, freezing drizzle, freezing fog, low visibility, and light snowfall developed over eastern New Mexico and the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. Total ice accumulations of near one tenth of an inch impacted much of eastern New Mexico along with one to three inches of snow. Locally higher amounts up to five inches were reported around Angel Fire and Eagle Nest. The combination of more record cold temperatures with ice and snow led to difficult driving conditions across much of northeast New Mexico. U.S. Highway 64 was closed between Clayton and Raton due to very icy conditions and at least two crashes, one near Clayton and another near Des Moines. Interstate 40 was closed along a section between Newkirk and Tucumcari where at least one semi trailer overturned and another vehicle left the roadway.  The northbound lane of U.S. Highway 285 was closed near Vaughn due to very icy travel and at least one vehicle accident. Interstate 25 was also closed due to multiple vehicle crashes in black ice. The number of vehicle crashes was estimated at eight and the extent of any injuries was unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northbound lanes of U.S. Highway 285 were closed near Vaughn due to severe winter travel conditions, including at least one vehicle accident. Damage amounts and the extent of any injuries was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of tropical convection along the west coast of Mexico during the final days of September 2019 organized into Tropical Storm Narda through October 1, 2019. Meanwhile, an upper level low pressure system shifted south along the west coast of the United States allowing atmospheric flow to become southerly over the desert southwest. A record high level of atmospheric moisture for the month of October that surged north into New Mexico in this pattern led to widespread showers and thunderstorms with torrential rainfall over southeast New Mexico. One day rainfall totals averaged two to four inches with locally heavier amounts in excess of six inches from southern Chaves County northeastward into Roosevelt and Curry counties. Flash flooding impacted much of southern Chaves County where communities along U.S. 285 were inundated by flood waters. Flash flooding also occurred around Clovis and Portales as playa lakes overflowed onto nearby roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roswell airport set a new daily record maximum rainfall amount of 3.44 for October 1st, breaking the previous record of 1.54 from 1998. Six to eight inches of water flooded a home on the south side of Roswell. Significant damage occurred to flooring, drywall, furniture, and other personal items. The entire family of seven was displaced into a hotel for several nights. Damage amounts are estimated for significant damage to the home and loss of most items inside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second tap of abundant moisture in association with the remnants of Tropical Storm Narda moved northeast across western Mexico into the desert southwest on October 3, 2019. Antecedent conditions from the record rainfall and flash flooding on October 1st set the stage for more significant flooding across southeast New Mexico. Widespread rainfall amounts of one to three inches were common with this second event. Localized heavier amounts in excess of three inches were reported with several daily records broken. Flash flooding once again impacted areas of southern Chaves County where more homes and roads were inundated by flood waters. Clovis and Portales also saw another round of flooding. Rainfall amounts for the first three days of October across parts of southeast New Mexico exceeded precipitation totals for the entire summer season. Central New Mexico was also able to pick up some heavy rainfall with this event. Locations from near Los Lunas to Albuquerque and Santa Fe picked up between one half inch and one inch of rain in less than 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer 13 miles north of Clovis recorded a new daily record rainfall of 3.68 on October 3rd. Minor flooding was reported throughout the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist, southerly flow at the surface combined with an upper level trough moving into the Great Basin to create a favorable atmosphere for heavy rain. A strong thunderstorm developed over the Truth or Consequences area and dropped over 2 inches of rain in an hour causing localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 2 inches of rain fell between 5 PM and 6 PM causing flash flooding to occur around Truth or Consequences and Williamsburg.  Water and debris covered portions of Highway 187 making the road impassable. Las Palomas Canyon Road was also flooded. One water rescue was required.  Also, the Ralph Edwards Park and Golf Course became flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions across portions of western New Mexico at the end of September 2019 expanded to include northern New Mexico in October. Moderate to severe drought conditions expanded to include 35 percent of the state of which 9 percent across western NM was deemed as severe drought. Abnormally dry to moderate drought conditions in southeastern NM at the end of September improved to no drought conditions in October as the remnants of Tropical Storm Narda moved through the area. Precipitation amounts across western NM during October ranged from a quarter to a half inch, however, dismal precipitation amounts continued in the Four Corners region where less than a quarter inch of precipitation was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions spread to more of the Northwest Plateau through October 2019. The Farmington Airport only recorded 0.17 inches of precipitation during the month, well below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was moving from southern Arizona to New Mexico. This allowed instability to increase across southeast New Mexico. Wind shear and low-level moisture also increased as a low-level jet stream moved over the area. These conditions resulted in a storm with strong winds developing across southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Lea County and produced a 59 mph wind gust two miles southwest of Tatum at the mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was over the region. A cold front moved into the Texas Panhandle later in the day. These conditions, along with moisture across the area, allowed for thunderstorms to develop. One storm produced large hail in southeast New Mexico.

An upper level trough was over the western half of the country with a ridge over the eastern half. Moisture and strong daytime heating lead to instability across southeast New Mexico. Sufficient wind shear was also present. These conditions contributed to thunderstorms with large hail across southeast New Mexico.

Tropical Storm Narda was in the eastern Pacific near the west coast of Mexico. This storm provided southeast New Mexico with a rich supply of moisture, and there was lift over the region. These conditions produced heavy rain across southeast New Mexico which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Eddy County and produced flash flooding. There was a high water rescue on Lakewood Road about 13 miles south of Artesia. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale outflow boundary from thunderstorms over west Texas pushed west into eastern New Mexico during the afternoon of July 10, 2019. A significant increase in moisture behind this moisture interacted with strong afternoon heating and abundant instability to produce isolated showers and thunderstorms. A couple of these storms became strong with heavy rainfall, hail, gusty winds, and frequent lightning. The strongest storms impacted the area between Tucumcari and Clayton where hail up to the size of nickels was reported. Strong canyon winds were reported within the Albuquerque metro area as the boundary moved west into the Rio Grande Valley. Small tree limbs were downed in parts of the city from near the University of New Mexico to the Albuquerque Sunport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell a few miles north of Rosebud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rich plume of moisture in place over eastern New Mexico on July 21, 2019 allowed widespread showers and thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall to impact much of the area through July 22, 2019. Several clusters of thunderstorms with rainfall amounts between one and two inches moved repeatedly over areas of eastern New Mexico during the early morning hours of July 22nd. Minor flooding was reported in several areas however the most significant rainfall impacted Union County where three to four inches of rain was reported. One vehicle was swept off a state highway south of Clayton and fortunately no one was killed or injured. Later in the day on the 22nd a large complex of storms moved slowly south through the Cabezon and Chamisa wilderness areas of western Sandoval County. The Rio Puerco at Guadalupe rose nearly 14 feet in response to the torrential rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced flash flooding along state road 402 around Amistad. One vehicle was washed off the roadway but fortunately no one was killed or injured. The vehicle appeared to have suffered only minor water damage and amounts are a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the early afternoon of September 7, 2019. This activity moved slowly east with heavy rainfall, small hail, and strong winds. The Ute Park burn scar experienced minor flooding with sharp rises also reported on the Cimarron River. The strongest storm impacted the area around the Raton airport where a peak wind gust up to 67 mph was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust up to 67 mph at the Raton airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and unstable airmass with meager moisture over northern New Mexico set the stage for a couple showers and thunderstorms on September 1, 2019. A large dust devil kicked up on the edge of one of these showers near Taos during the late afternoon hours. The dust devil damaged the roof of the Medley restaurant as it moved rapidly through the property. Fortunately, there were no injuries with this dust devil event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large dust devil moved across state road 150 and over the Medley restaurant north of Taos. A large section of roofing was torn off the restaurant. The restaurant was closed for several days. Fortunately there were no injuries. Damage amounts have been estimated based on news footage of the dust devil.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An easterly wave that drifted northwest through northern Mexico on September 12-13, 2019 moved slowly north into southern New Mexico on September 14, 2019. A deep tap of tropical moisture in association with the easterly wave along with weak steering flow over the region produced slow-moving thunderstorms with torrential rainfall. The heaviest rainfall impacted Socorro County where several storms that dumped between two to three inches of rainfall resulted in flash flooding. Road damage and closures were reported around Abeytas and Alamo between 5 and 6 pm. Heavy rainfall and small hail was also reported around Albuquerque and Rio Rancho during the evening hours with some locations picking up a quick one to 1.5 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported damaged roads and culverts with closures in the area south of Alamo. Damage amounts are a rough estimate for dirt road and culvert wash outs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure centered over eastern New Mexico with sufficient low level moisture and instability led to isolated showers and thunderstorms over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. An area of showers developed just to the southwest of Ute Park and drifted slowly northeast over the burn scar south of town. Rainfall amounts of one quarter to one half inch led to flash flooding. A large area of rocks, mud, and debris flooded the same area impacted by several flash flood in 2018 along Eagle Lane. Impacts from flood waters to any structures in the area was minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large rocks, mud, and debris flooded Saw Mill Canyon. Impacts to structures was minimal but Eagle Lane and Hummingbird Lane were impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The axis of monsoon moisture shifted over central New Mexico on August 2, 2019 while a weak boundary moved southwest through the eastern plains. The combination of deep moisture, strong heating, and abundant instability with weak steering flow led to scattered showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall and strong outflow winds. Wet microbursts impacted the area around Clayton and Moriarty with peak wind gusts near 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet microburst produced a peak wind gust up to 59 mph at the Clayton airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep tap of monsoon moisture in place over New Mexico produced numerous showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall on August 3, 2019. Slow-moving thunderstorms produced rainfall amounts in excess of one inch in many locations, including parts of the Albuquerque metro area. Some of the stronger storms during the late afternoon produced hail in additional to heavy rainfall and gusty winds. A strong storm near Lemitar dropped penny size hail. A thunderstorm that sat over Water Canyon west of Socorro produced flash flooding with large rocks and mud across the roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of pennies near Lemitar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of four hikers was descending a trail when a thunderstorm quickly intensified and lightning struck two of the four hikers. One of the hikers, Bruce Ponder, was blown off a nearby rock cliff and landed in a tree with minor injuries. The other hiker, Don Harrington, sustained serious injuries from the strike. The hikers were airlifted to UNMH where they both fortunately lived through the ordeal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure accompanied by very dry air  moved into west Texas and southeast New Mexico resulting in record breaking triple digit temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high temperature for the day reached 110 degrees at the Paducah RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture over New Mexico interacted with a back door cold front shifting southwest through the northeast plains. An area of showers and thunderstorms moving slowly south from Colorado along the Sangre de Cristo Mountains into the Ute Park area on August 5, 2019. A brief heavy storm produced a quick one quarter inch to four tenths of an inch of rainfall above Ute Park. Flash flooding surged down into the same area of Ute Park along Eagle Creek and Hummingbird Lane that has been repeatedly impacted by flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A surge of water 5 to 6 feet deep impacted Eagle Creek Lane and Hummingbird Lane within Ute Park. Flood waters came within an inch of moving into one home in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of monsoon moisture centered over New Mexico interacted with a surface boundary moving westward across the eastern plains on August 6, 2019. Thunderstorms erupted over the Sangre de Cristo and Jemez Mountains during the early afternoon before drifting southward into the Rio Grande Valley. The area between Santa Fe, Cochiti, and Rio Rancho was impacted particularly hard with localized rainfall amounts up to two inches in around one hour. Torrential rainfall impacted Los Cerrillos where many roads were washed out with rocks, mud, and debris. Tent Rocks was closed for a couple days due to roads damaged by flood waters, rocks, and debris. Rio Rancho also experienced flash flooding with many roads on the far west side of town inundated by water and mud. Damage amounts are estimated for impacts to roadways in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tent Rocks National Monument was closed for two days due to washed out roads from flood waters. Rocks, mud, and debris covered several sections of the main road to the monument. Damage amounts are estimated for roadways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets turned into rivers near 6th St NE and 5th Ave NE. Damage amounts are estimated for roadways.

A dramatic video along Goldmine Road south of Cerrillos looked like a tropical storm was impacting the area. Damage amounts are estimated for road damage in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and instability in place over eastern New Mexico on August 8, 2019 led to isolated slow-moving showers and thunderstorms. Most of these storms produced heavy rainfall, gusty winds, and small hail. The strongest storms impacted the area near Capulin where half dollar size hail and flash flooding was reported. Significant damage occurred to roads leading to the Capulin Volcano. The area around Rosebud also reported nickel size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail reported at Capulin Volcano National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that developed along a cold front moving quickly east through eastern New Mexico resulted in heavy rainfall, strong winds, and large hail over parts of Guadalupe County on September 20, 2019. An observer 15 miles southeast of Santa Rosa reported hail up to the size of quarters. No damage occurred with the hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported east of U.S. Highway 84 several miles southeast of Santa Rosa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable airmass in place along the Front Range of the Rockies on August 13, 2019 set the stage for an active night of severe weather over northeastern New Mexico through the morning hours of August 14, 2019. A large complex of showers and thunderstorms first developed over eastern Colorado before shifting southward down the eastern plains of New Mexico. The strongest storms impacted the area from near Clayton southward to Nara Visa where hail, high winds, frequent lightning, and torrential rainfall were reported. The most significant impacts occurred around Nara Visa where quarter size hail, severe winds, and three inches of rainfall resulted in damage to roofs and trees in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail and high winds damaged the roof of at least one home in Nara Visa. Damage amounts were included in the associated high wind report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and persistent area of upper level high pressure over the Great Basin region starting in June 2019 delivered very hot and dry conditions to the Four Corners area throughout the summer of 2019. The area had just recovered from an extended period of exceptional drought earlier in the spring so it did not take much time with well below normal precipitation to develop drought over the area once again. Precipitation amounts at several locations were close to zero for the three month stretch. In many cases this was the driest stretch on record for the Four Corners region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed during August 2019. The Farmington airport received  a record low total precipitation amount of 0.05 for July and August 2019.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms that developed over eastern Lincoln County during the afternoon of September 11, 2019 moved east into southern Chaves County and produced wind damage shortly around 4 pm. Local fire officials reported uprooted trees and downed power poles in the area of State Road 2 between Darby and Orchard Park roads. Seven to eight power poles were downed in the area around Midway and Cherokee roads. One resident in the area said his travel camper and a carport were completely destroyed by the winds. Fortunately, no injuries or traffic accidents were reported due to the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds produced damage in the area around State Road 2 in Midway. Several trees were uprooted, seven to eight power poles were downed, one travel camper and one carport were destroyed. Damage amounts are estimated for property damage and downed power poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved across New Mexico and West Texas briefly causing strong winds in the Guadalupe Mountains.
A deep upper trough developed over the western U.S. Strong winds ahead of this trough mixed into the higher elevations of the Guadalupe Mountains.
A quick moving upper trough brought strong winds to the Guadalupe and Delaware Mountains.

Moderate to severe drought conditions across portions of western New Mexico at the end of August 2019 continued in September. Moderate to severe drought conditions expanded to 33 percent of the state of which 9 percent was deemed as severe drought in the Four Corners area. Precipitation amounts across western NM during September ranged from one to two inches except in the Four Corners region which observed about half an inch or less of rain. Drought conditions continued to worsen due to these below average precipitation amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed over western McKinley County and extreme western Cibola County during September 2019. Unusually dry weather during July and August set the stage for deteriorating drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system that brought light snowfall to much of northern and western New Mexico on November 25, 2019 dragged a potent cold front through the state on November 26, 2019. Strong west winds during the early morning along the east slopes of the central mountain chain spread eastward through the early afternoon with wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph. The strongest winds impacted the Caprock region where Clovis recorded a peak wind gust up to 68 mph. Fortunately, no damage or injuries were reported with this wind event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periodic sustained winds up to 40 mph were reported at Clines Corners.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low was centered over northern Nevada with a 150 knot jet streak moving through southern New Mexico.  A surface low was over western Colorado with an associated cold front moving through the region. Strong southwest winds ahead of the front and west-northwest winds behind the front gusted up to 76 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station one mile south of Sunspot recorded a peak gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system that moved slowly northeast from Arizona into New Mexico on December 27, 2019 produced light to moderate mountain snow with valley rain. A couple strong thunderstorms with small hail and heavy rain also developed over eastern New Mexico with this system. One of these thunderstorms developed into a supercell and produced an exceptionally rare tornado over rural north-central Curry County near the Palla West Dairy. This was the latest confirmed tornado on record for the state of New Mexico. A second upper level low pressure system developed over the region in the wake of this first system on December 28, 2019. The second system was accompanied by a back door cold front over eastern New Mexico. Heavier mountain snow impacted the northern and western high terrain with much colder temperatures and very difficult travel conditions. Snowfall amounts ranged from 12 to 20 inches over the northern mountains with lighter amounts of two to six inches over the lower terrain of northern and western New Mexico. Numerous motor vehicle accidents were reported along Interstate 40 between Gallup and Grants as well as along U.S. Highway 285 north of Santa Fe. The extent of any injuries or damage to vehicles was unavailable from New Mexico State Police.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 18 inches in the higher terrain from near Red River to Taos Ski Valley. Difficult travel conditions were reported along U.S. Highway 64 and State Road 38.

The extent of any injuries or damage to vehicles was unavailable from New Mexico State Police.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to 22 inches in the higher terrain east of Santa Fe. Difficult travel conditions were reported over higher mountain roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions across portions of western and northern New Mexico at the end of November 2019 slightly improved across portions of northern and central NM in December. Moderate to severe drought conditions improved to 28 percent of the state of which 15 percent across western and northern NM was deemed as severe drought. December 2019 saw an active pattern impact much of NM, but only light to moderate snow amounts resulted in a month that was drier than normal. Precipitation amounts across western New Mexico during December ranged from one half inch to one inch. Areas across central and eastern NM generally received amounts ranging from one half to three quarters of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from November 2019 continued in December.

December 2019 saw an active pattern impact much of NM, but only light to moderate snow amounts resulted in a month that was drier than normal. Precipitation amounts across western New Mexico during December ranged from one half inch to one inch. Areas across central and eastern NM generally received amounts ranging from one half to three quarters of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from November 2019 continued in December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system that moved slowly northeast from Arizona into New Mexico on December 27, 2019 produced light to moderate mountain snow with valley rain. A couple strong thunderstorms with small hail and heavy rain also developed over eastern New Mexico with this system. One of these thunderstorms developed into a supercell and produced an exceptionally rare tornado over rural north-central Curry County near the Palla West Dairy. This was the latest confirmed tornado on record for the state of New Mexico. A second upper level low pressure system developed over the region in the wake of this first system on December 28, 2019. The second system was accompanied by a back door cold front over eastern New Mexico. Heavier mountain snow impacted the northern and western high terrain with much colder temperatures and very difficult travel conditions. Snowfall amounts ranged from 12 to 20 inches over the northern mountains with lighter amounts of two to six inches over the lower terrain of northern and western New Mexico. Numerous motor vehicle accidents were reported along Interstate 40 between Gallup and Grants as well as along U.S. Highway 285 north of Santa Fe. The extent of any injuries or damage to vehicles was unavailable from New Mexico State Police.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 2 to 8 inches along the Interstate 40 corridor from near Fort Wingate to Prewitt led to severe travel conditions with low visibility. Several motor vehicles accidents were reported however no details were available from NMSP on any damages or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved through the central and southern Rockies with an associated surface cold front.  As the front moved through, upslope flow developed over the Sacramento Mountains with a period of heavy snow across the highest elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall around the Cloudcroft area ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent jet stream moving over New Mexico on December 13, 2019 produced strong northwest winds along the east slopes of the central mountain chain. Areas from near Angel Fire south to Las Vegas, Clines Corners, and Corona reported wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph. The strongest winds however impacted Lincoln County where gusts near 60 mph occurred during the late morning hours. An observer near Arabela estimated peak wind gusts near 70 mph toppled an old oak tree on his property. No other damage was reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts estimated at 70 mph toppled an old oak tree six inches from the base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low pressure system over the Great Basin on December 14, 2019 moved slowly southeast into northern New Mexico through December 15, 2019. A frigid back door cold front sagged southwest into the eastern plains during this period and set the stage for snow across the northern high terrain and parts of the northeast plains. The heaviest snowfall impacted area from near Chama to Red River and Angel Fire where six to 14 inches was reported. Parts of the Interstate 25 corridor around Raton eastward along U.S. 64 picked up two to five inches. Impacts were mostly relegated to the higher terrain where difficult travel conditions occurred. Very cold air moved into the entire region in the wake of this system with low temperatures in the single digits and teens. Angel Fire fell to a whopping 26 degrees below zero on the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches from near Chama eastward into the higher terrain of eastern Rio Arriba County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather setup impacted New Mexico over the 2019 Thanksgiving holiday week. The first system off the coast of Baja California moved into the desert southwest on November 27, 2019, and anomalously high subtropical moisture streamed northward into the state. Precipitation initially formed over southern New Mexico in the form of rain, but as surface temperatures and snow levels fell overnight, it quickly changed over to snow. Warmer air aloft even allowed for a period of freezing rain across east central New Mexico early in the morning of the 28th. As temperatures warmed above freezing on the 28th, snow changed over to light rain for many areas. A second, stronger storm system originating from the Pacific northwest then moved southward into the Great Basin area on November 28th and crossed the central Rockies on the 29th. A strong Pacific cold front brought another round of cold temperatures and lower snow levels that allowed for another round of snow across the western and northern high terrain on the 29th. Hazardous travel conditions and vehicle accidents forced road closures along portions of Interstates 25 and 40 on Thanksgiving Day. The Albuquerque metro area even set a new daily record snow amount during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches from Arabela to near Corona.

A potent storm system originating over the Pacific northwest moved southward into the Great Basin on November 21, 2019 and then crossed the central Rocky Mountains on November 22, 2019. Strong lift associated with the upper level low pressure system combined with a very cold airmass to bring widespread snow to western and northern New Mexico. The highest snow accumulations occurred in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains where the highest totals ranged from six to twelve inches, and some locations over 9,500 feet received over a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various reporting stations across the area picked up 12 to 18 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system that brought light snowfall to much of northern and western New Mexico on November 25, 2019 dragged a potent cold front through the state on November 26, 2019. Strong west winds during the early morning along the east slopes of the central mountain chain spread eastward through the early afternoon with wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph. The strongest winds impacted the Caprock region where Clovis recorded a peak wind gust up to 68 mph. Fortunately, no damage or injuries were reported with this wind event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained wind speeds between 40 mph and 52 mph were reported for several hours around Clovis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions across portions of western and northern New Mexico at the end of October 2019 continued to expand into central NM in November. Moderate to severe drought conditions expanded to include 45 percent of the state of which 18 percent across western and northern NM was deemed as severe drought. An active pattern during the second half of November brought several winter storms and welcome moisture to NM in the form of rain and snow. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was over the Southern Plains with an upper level trough over the west coast. An upper level disturbance moved over southeast New Mexico and West Texas. A dryline developed across the area with good moisture to the east of the dryline. Good wind shear and instability were present. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms that produced hail in southeast New Mexico.

An upper level trough was moving from over the Rocky Mountains to southeast New Mexico and West Texas which provided lift. A dryline was present across the area with good moisture to the east of this feature. Strong instability and wind shear were also present. A cold front was also beginning to move into the area during the evening hours. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms developing with large hail, strong winds, and a tornado across West Texas and southeast New Mexico.
